source: This Week in Startups — AI platform shift, Nvidia outlook, extinction risk
summary: GPU utilization efficiency is dramatically underoptimized (30% utilization during GPT-3/Palm training), and AI-driven optimization of this scarce resource will unlock significant effective compute gains without new hardware.
summary_extended: Baker argues that GPU utilization during the training of large language models like GPT-3 and PaLM was only approximately 30% of the time, based on published papers from OpenAI and Google, because software engineers were not accustomed to optimizing code for scarce and expensive compute resources. As H100s become increasingly scarce and costly, Baker contends that economic pressure will force developers to dramatically improve utilization efficiency, and that AI itself will be deployed to solve this optimization problem. The implication is that significant effective compute capacity gains can be unlocked through software improvements alone, without requiring additional hardware procurement. Baker frames this as an underappreciated dynamic in the AI infrastructure landscape, though he stops short of making a direct, specific investment call tied to this observation. The thesis is more of a structural observation about the compute efficiency frontier than a direct buy/sell signal on any single company.
detail: Baker cites OpenAI and Google papers showing GPUs were only utilized 30% of the time during training of their large models, because programmers weren't accustomed to optimizing for scarce compute. As H100s become scarce and expensive, optimization pressure will increase, and AI itself will be used to solve the utilization problem — potentially yielding large effective capacity gains.
confidence_evidence: Baker cites specific data (30% utilization from published papers) and frames this as an underappreciated dynamic, but doesn't make a specific investment call from it.
quote_fragment: the gpus they used to train them were only utilized 30 percent of the time

ticker_audit.reasoning: While NVDA manufactures the H100 GPUs referenced in the thesis, the core claim is about software-driven utilization optimization — not about GPU demand or Nvidia's revenue. Better utilization could theoretically reduce the need for incremental GPU purchases, making Nvidia's relationship to this thesis ambiguous at best. Baker makes no investment call on NVDA from this observation, and the thesis does not unambiguously benefit or harm Nvidia specifically.
